sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 239, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1. Pink 6x6 character at left edge of a dark room. Three 4x4 colored blocks on the right wall: red (top, row 19-22), blue (middle, row 25-28), green/N (bottom, row 31-34). A white tile path extends right from the character into the room. Bottom HUD bar shows order: pink, red, green, blue.
- Goal model: Visit/touch the colored blocks in the order shown on the bottom bar: red, then green, then blue.
- Action model: RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN move the character. Need to probe movement.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to enter the room, then navigate to the red block first.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 238 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 3.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key discovery! The first frame had:
- Character at rows 36-41
- Blocks at R=19, b=25, N=31
So initially, blocks were at R=19-22, b=25-28, N=31-34. The character was at 36-41.
Now blocks are at R=13-16, b=19-22, N=25-28. The character is at 36-41.
The blocks shifted UP by 6 rows! This happened because I used ACTION7 from 24-29 (overlapping with green), which shifted blocks up.
So the initial state was:
- Character: 36-41
- Blocks: R=19-22, b=25-28, N=31-34
- Path: rows 38-39
And I moved the character UP to 30-35, then to 24-29, then used ACTION7 which shifted blocks up by 6 rows.
Now blocks are at R=13-16, b=19-22, N=25-28. The character is at 36-41.
The path is at rows 38-39.
Wait, but the blocks are now ABOVE the character. The character is at 36-41, and the lowest block (green/N) is at 25-28. So there's no overlap.
Now I understand the mechanic better:
- ACTION7 from a position overlapping a block shifts all blocks up by 6 rows
- This also moves the character up by 6 rows (to the next slot)
- The character can move UP/DOWN to different slots (6-row increments)
- Blocks block movement to overlapping slots
So initially, the blocks were at R=19, b=25, N=31. The character was at 36. The character could move UP to 30, then to 24 (overlapping green at 31-34... wait, 24-29 doesn't overlap with 31-34).
Wait, let me recalculate. Initially:
- Character: 36-41
- Green block: 31-34
- If character moves UP to 30-35, it overlaps with green at 31-34 (rows 31-34 are within 30-35)
- If character moves UP to 24-29, it doesn't overlap with green at 31-34
So initially, the character could move UP from 36-41 to 30-35, which overlaps with green at 31-34. Then from 30-35, the character could use ACTION7 to shift blocks up and move to 24-29.
But I already did this! And now blocks are at R=13, b=19, N=25, and character is at 36-41.
The problem is: I shifted blocks up too far. Now green is at 25-28, and the character can't reach 24-29 because green blocks it.
Wait, but the character is at 36-41. Can it move UP to 30-35? Let me check: green is at 25-28, which doesn't overlap with 30-35. So the character CAN move to 30-35.
From 30-35, can the character move UP to 24-29? Green is at 25-28, which overlaps with 24-29 (rows 25-28 are within 24-29). So the character CANNOT move to 24-29.
So I'm stuck again. But wait - what if I can shift blocks DOWN somehow? Or what if I need to work with the current block positions?
Let me think about what the goal might be. The bottom bar shows: pink, red, green, blue. Current block order: red (top), blue (middle), green (bottom). Target order: red, green, blue.
What if I need to rearrange the blocks to match the bottom bar order? Currently: R, b, N. Target: R, N, b.
What if the mechanic is:
1. Move character to align with a block
2. Press RIGHT to "grab" the block
3. Move character to a new position
4. Press LEFT to "release" the block
Or what if I need to use ACTION7 to shift blocks until they're in the right order?
Actually, let me reconsider. What if the goal is to "collect" blocks in the order shown on the bottom bar? And collecting a block removes it from the wall?
Let me try: from 36-41, move UP to 30-35, then try to interact with the green block at 25-28.
Wait, but the path at 32-33 doesn't overlap with green at 25-28. So I can't interact with green from 30-35.
Hmm, what if I need to use ACTION7 from 30-35 to toggle to 36-41, then do something else?
Actually, let me reconsider the whole approach. Maybe I need to:
1. Reset the level somehow (go back to initial state)
2. Then play optimally
But I don't know how to reset. Let me check if there's an undo or reset action.
Actually, let me try a different approach. What if I need to use LEFT to shorten the path? Let me try LEFT from 36-41.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Character is at 36-41, blocks at R=13-16, b=19-22, N=25-28
# Path is at rows 38-39
# Let me try LEFT to shorten the path, then see what happens

action(['LEFT'])
print(f"After LEFT from 36-41:")
print(f"  gameplay_changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

# Check path
path_nodes = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'w' and n['boundary'][0][1] >= 10 and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
print(f"  Path nodes: {len(path_nodes)}")
if path_nodes:
    all_cols = []
    for n in path_nodes:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        all_cols.extend(cols)
    print(f"  Path cols: {min(all_cols)}-{max(all_cols)}")

# Check character
char_rows = [r for r in range(10, 45) if 'M' in ascii_lines[r][:30]]
print(f"  Char rows: {char_rows[:2]}...")

# Now try: UP to 30-35, then RIGHT to extend path
action(['UP', 'RIGHT'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
char_rows = [r for r in range(10, 45) if 'M' in ascii_lines[r][:30]]
print(f"\nAfter UP, RIGHT:")
print(f"  Char rows: {char_rows[:2]}...")
path_nodes = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'w' and n['boundary'][0][1] >= 10 and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
if path_nodes:
    all_cols = []
    for n in path_nodes:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        all_cols.extend(cols)
    print(f"  Path cols: {min(all_cols)}-{max(all_cols)}")
print(f"  gameplay_changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  gameplay_changed: True, no_impact: False
  Path nodes: 9
  Path cols: 16-40
  Char rows: [36, 37]...

After UP, RIGHT:
  Char rows: [30, 31]...
  Path cols: 16-46
  gameplay_changed: True, no_impact: False
